topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop hummed with the usual midday bustle, but my focus kept drifting. Liam was recounting his latest project with such animated enthusiasm, his hands gesturing wildly as he described the intricate coding he was tackling. I nodded along, offering polite interjections, but my eyes kept snagging on the way he avoided mentioning any specific clients or company structure. It was a small thing, a flicker of something amiss, but it settled in my stomach like a cold stone. When he finally paused, wiping a bead of sweat from his temple, I decided to press. "So, where exactly are you working these days, Liam? You mentioned a big tech firm last week." His smile tightened, a fraction too quickly, and he deflected with a breezy, "Oh, just freelance gigs right now. Keeping things flexible." The air between us suddenly felt thick, charged with unspoken words I couldn't quite decipher. I watched him, waiting for the facade to crack, but it remained firmly in place.

[story 2]

I found myself staring at the resume Liam had casually dropped on my desk. It was a polished document, detailing a position at a prestigious marketing agency. Yet, the details felt hollow when I compared them to the vague, generalized descriptions he’d given me over the past few months. A persistent knot formed beneath my ribs, tightening with every glance at the paper. I tried to keep my expression neutral, focusing instead on organizing the files around me, but my fingers kept drumming a restless rhythm on the edge of the desk. Later that evening, when he mentioned needing to cover an unexpected expense, his explanation sounded rehearsed, lacking the genuine urgency I expected. I leaned back in my chair, my gaze fixed on the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of meaningless color. Every sound seemed amplified—the distant sirens, the clatter of dishes—and I felt a distinct pressure building behind my eyes.

[story 3]

It started subtly, a mismatch in the details. Liam spoke of working on complex client integrations, but when I asked about the specific software stack, he became vague, stumbling over technical terms. A low thrum of apprehension began to vibrate through my chest. I found myself scrutinizing his answers, cataloging inconsistencies like a detective examining flawed evidence. One afternoon, while we were ostensibly studying for a shared class, he mentioned a specific project deadline that I distinctly remembered him saying was impossible given his current workload. His eyes darted away, and he cleared his throat repeatedly. The silence that followed was heavy, suffocating. I shifted in my seat, pulling my sweater tighter around me, acutely aware of the tension radiating off him. I didn't say anything then; I just let the silence stretch, watching his carefully constructed composure begin to fray at the edges.
